messages.content: You are a senior HVAC knowledge-base reviewer. Validate extracted knowledge objects from one equipment manual. Accept only useful, grounded, reviewable operational knowledge supported by the evidence. Reject marketing claims, UI-only navigation, isolated terminal labels, pure part names, duplicate/noisy items, generic prose with no operational value, or items whose evidence does not support the structured payload. Supported knowledge_type values: fault_code, parameter_spec, performance_spec, maintenance_procedure, application_guidance, operational_sequence, fault_diagnostic_rule, diagnostic_step, symptom. Reply with strict JSON only.

Manual filename: auto_ashrae2024.pdf
Equipment class: valve

Review every candidate below. Preserve candidate_id exactly.
Allowed category_if_not values: null, marketing, ui_behavior, terminal_label, part_name, duplicate, unsupported_type, weak_evidence, other.

{"candidates": [{"candidate_id": "cand_887d5e209983956e", "knowledge_type": "parameter_spec", "title": "Valve Authority Range for HVAC Coil Applications", "summary": "Valve authorities in the range of 30 to 50% are adequate for HVAC coil applications, with higher Δt coils requiring less valve authority. Authorities less than 30% should be avoided.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "valve_authority", "value_range": "30 to 50%", "unit": "%", "title": "Valve Authority Range for HVAC Coil Applications", "summary": "Valve authorities in the range of 30 to 50% are adequate for HVAC coil applications, with higher Δt coils requiring less valve authority. • System integration with other building systems

training, and construction)
• Basis of design• HV AC system analysis and selection matrix• System narratives• Budget costs (first cost, operating cost, and energy cost)• Final recommendation(s)
A brief outline of each of the final selections should be provided
along with overall budget and/or cost per square metre. In addition,
HV AC systems deemed inappropriate should be noted as havingbeen considered but not found applicable to meet the owner’s pri-mary HVAC goal.
The report should include an HV AC system selection matrix that
identifies the one or two suggested HV AC system selections (pri -
mary and secondary, when applicable), system constraints, and
other constraints and considerations.", "source_pages": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6], "trust_level": "L4"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_41b796defc38912f", "knowledge_type": "parameter_spec", "title": "Low-Temperature Heating System Supply Water Temperature Range", "summary": "Low-temperature heating systems are generally designed with supply water temperatures from 80 to 115°C and temperature drops from 10 to 55 K.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "supply_water_temperature", "value_range": "80 to 115°C", "unit": "°C", "title": "Low-Temperature Heating System Supply Water Temperature Range", "summary": "Low-temperature heating systems are generally designed with supply water temperatures from 80 to 115°C and temperature drops from 10 to 55 K."}, "evidence": "1.4 2024 ASHRAE Handbook—HV AC Systems and Equipment  (SI)

construction or renovation because most codes prohibit combusti-
ble material in a ceiling return air plenum. Ceiling plenums andraised floors can also be used for supply air displacement systems tominimize horizontal distribution, along with other features dis -
cussed in Chapter 4.
Some ceiling plenum applications with lay-in panels do not work
well where the stack effect of a high-rise building or high-rise ele -
vators creates a negative pressure. If the plenum leaks to the low-pressure area, tiles may lift and drop out when the outside door isopened and closed.
The air temperature in a return air plenum directly below a roof
deck is usually higher by 2 to 3 K during the air-conditioning season
than in a ducted return. This can be an advantage to the occupiedspace below because heat gain to the space is reduced.", "source_pages": [2, 4, 5], "trust_level": "L4"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_1e5080395b215b16", "knowledge_type": "parameter_spec", "title": "AHRI Standard 410 Coil Rating Parameters", "summary": "Coils are rated within parameters: entering air dry-bulb 18 to 38°C, wet-bulb 15 to 30°C, air face velocity 1 to 4 m/s, evaporator refrigerant saturation temperature –1 to 13°C, entering chilled-water temperature 1.5 to 18°C, water velocity 0.3 to 2.4 m/s.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "coil_rating_parameters", "entering_air_dry_bulb": "18 to 38°C", "entering_air_wet_bulb": "15 to 30°C", "air_face_velocity": "1 to 4 m/s", "evaporator_refrigerant_saturation_temperature": "–1 to 13°C", "entering_chilled_water_temperature": "1.5 to 18°C", "water_velocity": "0.3 to 2.4 m/s", "title": "AHRI Standard 410 Coil Rating Parameters", "summary": "Coils are rated within parameters: entering air dry-bulb 18 to 38°C, wet-bulb 15 to 30°C, air face velocity 1 to 4 m/s, evaporator refrigerant saturation temperature –1 to 13°C, entering chilled-water temperature 1.5 to 18°C, water velocity 0.3 to 2.4 m/s."}, "evidence": "1.4 2024 ASHRAE Handbook—HV AC Systems and Equipment  (SI)

other constraints and considerations.", "source_pages": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6], "trust_level": "L4"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_a310d5c55ab6a30c", "knowledge_type": "parameter_spec", "title": "Typical Air Face Velocity for Dehumidifying Coils", "summary": "Dehumidifying coils for comfort applications are frequently selected in the range of 2.0 to 2.5 m/s air face velocity.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "air_face_velocity", "value_range": "2.0 to 2.5 m/s", "unit": "m/s", "title": "Typical Air Face Velocity for Dehumidifying Coils", "summary": "Dehumidifying coils for comfort applications are frequently selected in the range of 2.0 to 2.5 m/s air face velocity."}, "evidence": "For
information on calculating a dehumidi fying coil’s sensible heat ratio,
see the section on Performance of Dehumidifying Coils, or Appen-dix D of Cooling and Heating Load Calculation Principles  (Peder-
sen et al. 1998). For a given coil surface design and arrangement, therequired sensible heat ratio may be  satisfied by wide variations in
and combinations of air face velocity, in-tube temperature, flow rate,entering air temperature, coil depth, etc., although the variationsmay be self limiting. The maximum coil air face velocity should belimited to a value that prevents water carryover into the air ductwork.Dehumidifying coils for comfort applications are frequently selectedin the range of 2.0 to 2.5 m/s air face velocity.
Operating ratings of dehumidifyi ng coils for factory-assembled,
self-contained air conditioners are generally determined in conjunc-
tion with laboratory testing for the system capacity of the complete
unit assembly.", "source_pages": [5], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_f290d04f677b71eb", "knowledge_type": "parameter_spec", "title": "Standard Rating Point for Unitary Equipment", "summary": "A standard rating point has been 54 L/s per kilowatt of refrigeration effect, not to exceed 60 L/s per kilowatt for unitary equipment.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "standard_rating_point", "value": "54 L/s per kW, max 60 L/s per kW", "unit": "L/s per kW", "title": "Standard Rating Point for Unitary Equipment", "summary": "A standard rating point has been 54 L/s per kilowatt of refrigeration effect, not to exceed 60 L/s per kilowatt for unitary equipment."}, "evidence": "For example, a standard rating point has been
54 L/s per kilowatt of refrigeration effect, not to exceed 60 L/s perkilowatt for unitary equipment. Re frigerant (e.g., R-22) duty would
be 3 to 6 K superheat for an appr opriate balance at 7°C saturated
suction. For water coil s, circuitry would operate at 1.2 m/s, 5.5°C
inlet water, 6.7 K rise (or 36 mL /s per kilowatt of refrigeration
effect). The standard ratings at 26.7°C db and 19.5°C wb are repre-
sentative of the entering air conditions encountered in many com-
fort operations.", "source_pages": [5], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_de01dbd65c138caa", "knowledge_type": "parameter_spec", "title": "Refrigerant Superheat for Unitary Equipment Balance", "summary": "Refrigerant duty would be 3 to 6 K superheat for an appropriate balance at 7°C saturated suction.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "refrigerant_superheat", "value_range": "3 to 6 K", "unit": "K", "title": "Refrigerant Superheat for Unitary Equipment Balance", "summary": "Refrigerant duty would be 3 to 6 K superheat for an appropriate balance at 7°C saturated suction."}, "evidence": "For example, a standard rating point has been
54 L/s per kilowatt of refrigeration effect, not to exceed 60 L/s perkilowatt for unitary equipment. Re frigerant (e.g., R-22) duty would
be 3 to 6 K superheat for an appr opriate balance at 7°C saturated
suction. For water coil s, circuitry would operate at 1.2 m/s, 5.5°C
inlet water, 6.7 K rise (or 36 mL /s per kilowatt of refrigeration
effect). The standard ratings at 26.7°C db and 19.5°C wb are repre-
sentative of the entering air conditions encountered in many com-
fort operations.", "source_pages": [5], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_5a001e7481abbab1", "knowledge_type": "parameter_spec", "title": "Water Coil Circuitry Standard Conditions", "summary": "For water coils, circuitry would operate at 1.2 m/s, 5.5°C inlet water, 6.7 K rise (or 36 mL/s per kilowatt of refrigeration effect).", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "water_coil_standard_conditions", "velocity": "1.2 m/s", "inlet_water_temperature": "5.5°C", "temperature_rise": "6.7 K", "flow_rate": "36 mL/s per kW", "title": "Water Coil Circuitry Standard Conditions", "summary": "For water coils, circuitry would operate at 1.2 m/s, 5.5°C inlet water, 6.7 K rise (or 36 mL/s per kilowatt of refrigeration effect)."}, "evidence": "For example, a standard rating point has been
54 L/s per kilowatt of refrigeration effect, not to exceed 60 L/s perkilowatt for unitary equipment. Re frigerant (e.g., R-22) duty would
be 3 to 6 K superheat for an appr opriate balance at 7°C saturated
suction. For water coil s, circuitry would operate at 1.2 m/s, 5.5°C
inlet water, 6.7 K rise (or 36 mL /s per kilowatt of refrigeration
effect). The standard ratings at 26.7°C db and 19.5°C wb are repre-
sentative of the entering air conditions encountered in many com-
fort operations.", "source_pages": [5], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_6bcbf65871ae8b43", "knowledge_type": "parameter_spec", "title": "Standard Entering Air Conditions for Comfort Ratings", "summary": "The standard ratings at 26.7°C db and 19.5°C wb are representative of the entering air conditions encountered in many comfort operations.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "standard_entering_air_conditions", "dry_bulb": "26.7°C", "wet_bulb": "19.5°C", "title": "Standard Entering Air Conditions for Comfort Ratings", "summary": "The standard ratings at 26.7°C db and 19.5°C wb are representative of the entering air conditions encountered in many comfort operations."}, "evidence": "In completing this matrixassessment, the design engineer should identify the owner’s input tothe analysis. This input can also be applied as weighted multipliers,because not all criteria carry the same weighted value.
Many grading methods are available to complete an analytical
matrix analysis. Probably the simplest is to rate each item excellent,very good, good, fair, or poor. A numerical rating system such as 0to 10, with 10 equal to excellent and 0 equal to poor or not applica -
ble, can provide a quantitative result. The HV AC system with thehighest numerical value then becomes the recommended system toaccomplish the goal.
The system selection report should include a summary followed
by a more detailed account of the HV AC system analysis and systemselection. This summary should highlight key points and findingsthat led to the recommendation(s).", "source_pages": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6], "trust_level": "L4"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_14ef63b4795b3164", "knowledge_type": "parameter_spec", "title": "Standard Air Density for Airflow Ratings", "summary": "Airflow ratings are based on standard air of 1.20 kg/m³ at 20°C and a barometric pressure of 101.325 kPa.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "standard_air_density", "density": "1.20 kg/m³", "temperature": "20°C", "pressure": "101.325 kPa", "title": "Standard Air Density for Airflow Ratings", "summary": "Airflow ratings are based on standard air of 1.20 kg/m³ at 20°C and a barometric pressure of 101.325 kPa."}, "evidence": "In completing this matrixassessment, the design engineer should identify the owner’s input tothe analysis. This input can also be applied as weighted multipliers,because not all criteria carry the same weighted value.
Many grading methods are available to complete an analytical
matrix analysis. Probably the simplest is to rate each item excellent,very good, good, fair, or poor. A numerical rating system such as 0to 10, with 10 equal to excellent and 0 equal to poor or not applica -
ble, can provide a quantitative result. The HV AC system with thehighest numerical value then becomes the recommended system toaccomplish the goal.
The system selection report should include a summary followed
by a more detailed account of the HV AC system analysis and systemselection. This summary should highlight key points and findingsthat led to the recommendation(s).", "source_pages": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6], "trust_level": "L4"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_b7d13b88bf46d31c", "knowledge_type": "performance_spec", "title": "Freezing Condition for Dehumidification Coils", "summary": "Freezing does not occur with standard coils for comfort installations unless the refrigerant evaporating temperature at the coil outlet is below –4 to –2°C saturated.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "coil_freezing_condition", "temperature_range": "below –4 to –2°C saturated", "title": "Freezing Condition for Dehumidification Coils", "summary": "Freezing does not occur with standard coils for comfort installations unless the refrigerant evaporating temperature at the coil outlet is below –4 to –2°C saturated."}, "evidence": "In ho t, humid climates, the light-load
condition has a higher proportion of moisture and a correspond-
ingly lower proportion of sensible  heat. The result is higher dew
points in the conditioned spaces during light-load  conditions un-
less a special means for controlling inside dew points (e.g., reheator dual path) is used.
Fin surface freezing at light lo ads should be avoided. Freezing
occurs when a dehumidification coil’s surface temperature fallsbelow 0°C. Freezing does not occur with standard coils for comfort
installations unless the refrigerant evaporating temperature at the
coil outlet is below –4 to –2°C satu rated; the exact value depends on
the design of the coil, its opera ting dew point, and the amount of
loading. With coil and condensing units to balance at low tempera-
tures at peak loads (not a custom ary design choice), freezing may
occur when load suddenly decreases.", "source_pages": [5], "trust_level": "L3"}, {"candidate_id": "cand_7d2212edd5e80fac", "knowledge_type": "performance_spec", "title": "Airflow Resistance Reduction with Hydrophilic Coating", "summary": "Tests have shown as much as a 30% reduction in air pressure drop across a hydrophilic coil as opposed to a new untreated coil.", "structured_payload": {"parameter_name": "hydrophilic_coating_effect", "reduction": "up to 30%", "title": "Airflow Resistance Reduction with Hydrophilic Coating", "summary": "Tests have shown as much as a 30% reduction in air pressure drop across a hydrophilic coil as opposed to a new untreated coil."}, "evidence": "Therefore, final selection involves
economic balancing of the initial co st of the coil against the operat-
ing costs of the coil geometry co mbinations available to adequately
meet the performa nce requirements.
The aluminum fin surfaces of new dehumidifying coils tend to in-
hibit condensate sheeting action until they have aged for a year. Hy-
drophilic aluminum fin surface coatings reduce water droplet surface
tension, producing a more evenly dispersed wetted surface action at
initial start-up. Manufacturers have tried different methods of apply-
ing such coatings, including dippi ng the coil into a tank, coating the
fin stock material, or subjecting the material to a chemical etchingprocess. Tests have s hown as much as a 30% reduction in air pressure
drop across a hydrophilic coil as opposed a new untreated coil..", "source_pages": [5], "trust_level": "L3"}]}

Return JSON shape: {"verdicts":[{"candidate_id":"...","is_valid_hvac_knowledge":true,"reason":"short reason","category_if_not":null}]}
